Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I tried to dedicate about 26 hours a week, but with six online courses and everything else going on, it felt like I was constantly playing catch-up. I'd usually sit down in the evenings after work, make some coffee, and try to power through readings and videos. But my anxiety would spike and I'd find myself just staring at the screen, not really absorbing anything. I wanted to be more organized, but life kept getting in the way.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how overwhelmed I felt. Some weeks I'd try to stick to a schedule—Monday and Wednesday evenings, Saturday mornings—but then a deadline would hit and everything would collapse into panic mode. Being 28 and having other responsibilities meant I couldn't always protect my study time the way younger students might. Deadlines definitely drove most of my activity, not any consistent routine.
Q3: Usually it was either seeing a deadline notification or that gnawing anxiety that I was falling behind. I'd get an email reminder about an assignment and think, 'I need to at least log in and see what's happening.' Sometimes boredom would push me there too—like I knew I should be doing something productive, so I'd log in even if I wasn't really ready to engage. The guilt of knowing I was behind was a constant motivator, honestly.
Q4: There were a few weeks early on where logging in became almost automatic—part of my evening wind-down routine. I'd make dinner, then sit with my laptop and check the LMS like checking social media. But that didn't last long. Once assignments started piling up and my anxiety got worse, it became something I dreaded rather than a habit. The mindfulness exercises they had us do helped a bit, but the routine never fully stuck.
Q5: The reading materials and written content were most useful for me since that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and highlight them, make notes in the margins. The videos were helpful too—I watched over three hours of them—but honestly, I retained more from reading. Discussion boards had potential, but I only posted twice because the anxiety of putting my thoughts out there was overwhelming.
Q6: I basically ignored peer interactions entirely—zero interactions, which I'm not proud of. Group activities and discussion forums made my anxiety spike. I'd read other people's posts but couldn't bring myself to respond. I also delayed a lot of the optional practice quizzes because they felt like extra pressure when I was already drowning. If something wasn't explicitly required, it often got pushed to 'later' which meant never.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exam or assignments. With only 36% of assignments completed, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. If something seemed like it would help me understand a core concept or boost my grade, I'd prioritize it. But if it felt like busywork or supplementary, my brain would just check out and I'd feel bored and frustrated.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones came with deadlines and consequences, so they got done—or at least attempted. Optional stuff, even if interesting, always got bumped when stress hit. There's this weird guilt with optional activities too—you know they'd probably help, but when you're already failing to keep up with the mandatory stuff, adding more feels impossible. They just added to the mental load.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything I could and studied offline constantly. I'd print readings, take handwritten notes, review them on my commute. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer, with a notebook and highlighter. I also used external YouTube videos to clarify concepts when the course materials weren't clicking. None of that showed up in the system.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd spend hours reviewing downloaded materials, re-reading my notes, trying to understand concepts—but my LMS activity would show nothing. I'd access a PDF once, download it, and then study it for days offline. Or I'd watch a video, pause it constantly to take notes, but the system just sees one view. My real engagement was invisible to the platform.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a lecture document or video once, save it locally, and return to it multiple times over the following days. My internet isn't always reliable, so having offline copies was essential. I'd review the same material three or four times before an exam, but the LMS only captured that initial access. It made my engagement look way lower than it actually was.
Q12: Around midterms, everything fell apart. The workload from all six courses converged, my stress levels were through the roof, and my anxiety became paralyzing. I'd sit at my computer and just feel this weight pressing down on me. I started avoiding the LMS because logging in meant confronting how far behind I was. The boredom I felt was actually a defense mechanism—it was easier to feel nothing than to feel the panic.
Q13: Constantly. Fatigue was a huge factor—by the time I got home from my responsibilities, I was mentally drained. Confusion about assignment requirements made me procrastinate because I didn't know where to start. When I was stressed, I'd log in, look at everything I needed to do, get overwhelmed, and log out without doing anything productive. The competing demands of being a 28-year-old with a life outside school made everything harder.
Q14: More times than I can count. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. Physically present, mentally checked out. I'd watch videos at 2x speed just to mark them complete, not really processing anything. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph five times and still not remember what it said. My anxiety made it hard to actually engage even when I was technically 'active.'
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The bigger and more important something was, the more anxious it made me, and the more I'd avoid it. It's like my brain couldn't handle the pressure, so it just... shut down. I knew my assignment completion rate was terrible, but starting felt impossible sometimes. The mindfulness exercises helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was another story.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage—usually the night before or day of. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically around due dates. Without deadlines, I'd drift and procrastinate. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only thing that could cut through my anxiety and boredom enough to make me act.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. When I got constructive feedback, it motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But sometimes feedback came too late to be useful, or it was so critical that it just fed my anxiety and made me want to avoid the course altogether. I think I needed more encouragement along the way. The feedback loop felt broken—by the time I knew how I did, I was already behind on the next thing.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming with six online courses running simultaneously. The way content was organized in the LMS made it hard to prioritize—everything seemed equally important but I couldn't do it all. Having clearer weekly goals or a more guided pathway would have helped me stay on track. The open-ended structure gave me too much freedom, which with my low motivation just meant I floundered.
Q19: The sheer volume of materials made engagement harder. There was so much content that I felt paralyzed trying to figure out what to focus on. What made it easier was when modules had clear objectives and when readings were well-organized. The video content being downloadable helped. But the lack of structured check-ins or progress markers meant I could fall behind without realizing how bad it had gotten until it was too late.
Q20: That was literally me. I logged in 28 times, watched tons of video content, but only completed about a third of assignments. For me, it was the anxiety—I could passively consume content, but producing something to submit felt terrifying. What if it wasn't good enough? What if I failed? It was easier to keep watching and reading than to actually put myself out there. High activity doesn't mean high output when fear is involved.
Q21: I can see how that happens. Some people are just efficient—they download what they need, study offline, and perform well on exams without constant LMS presence. I tried to be that person but my exam score shows I wasn't successful. Still, a lot of my learning happened offline, so I understand how someone could have low visible activity but actually be engaged. The LMS doesn't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. That's useful for identifying who's completely absent. But it misses the quality of engagement entirely. It can't tell if someone's actually learning or just going through the motions. It misses offline study, emotional state, life circumstances. My high login frequency looked good on paper but didn't reflect my actual struggle.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material—not just completing tasks, but understanding concepts and wanting to learn more. It's when studying doesn't feel like a chore but like something meaningful. For me, true engagement was rare this semester. I was going through motions, fighting anxiety, trying to survive. Real engagement would have meant feeling curious instead of just stressed and bored.
Q24: The LMS couldn't show how hard I was fighting just to stay afloat. It couldn't capture the anxiety attacks before logging in, the hours spent with downloaded materials, the mindfulness exercises I did to calm myself enough to study. It couldn't show that I'm 28 with responsibilities that younger students don't have, or that my low resilience made every setback feel catastrophic. The data shows a failing student; it doesn't show someone who tried despite everything working against them.
